Q1: Honestly, my study routine was all over the place. I'm 27 and working part-time to make ends meet, so I couldn't stick to any consistent schedule. I'd usually try to squeeze in studying late at night or early mornings when I had energy, but with six online courses, it felt impossible to keep track of everything. I learn best by doing things hands-on, so just reading or watching videos never really stuck with me.
Q2: My pattern was completely dependent on deadlines and whatever crisis was happening that week. Some weeks I'd barely touch my coursework because of work shifts or just feeling overwhelmed. Then right before something was due, I'd panic and try to cram everything in. There was no regularity to it at all, which I know wasn't good for me.
Q3: Usually it was panic about an upcoming deadline that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a discussion post or a peer message and that would remind me the course existed. The peer support group I was connected with would sometimes nudge me to check in, which helped. But if nothing was immediately pressing, I'd forget about it for days.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. There were maybe a few weeks where I tried to make it a habit to log in every other day, but life kept getting in the way. The closest I got to routine was when my peer support partner would message me regularly, and I'd feel obligated to respond and check the materials. But that never lasted long enough to become automatic.
Q5: The discussion forums were actually helpful because I could interact with other students and that felt more real to me. I also liked any activities where I could actually do something practical rather than just read. The peer interactions kept me somewhat accountable. Videos were okay but I'd zone out if they were too long.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and longer videos. They just didn't hold my attention, and with my anxiety being what it is, I'd get overwhelmed looking at how much content there was. I'd tell myself I'd come back to them later but rarely did. The optional stuff was basically invisible to me because I was barely keeping up with required work.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it connected to something practical I could use. If I could interact with peers or actually apply what I was learning, that felt meaningful. Anything that was just passive consumption felt like a waste of time, even though I know it probably wasn't.
Q8: Optional activities might as well not have existed for me. I was so stressed trying to keep up with required work across six courses that anything labeled 'optional' got immediately deprioritized. Required activities at least had the pressure of grades attached, which was sometimes the only thing that got me moving. Optional stuff felt like a luxury I couldn't afford.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download PDFs and try to read them on my phone during bus rides to work. I'd also take handwritten notes because writing things out helps me remember better than typing. Sometimes I'd look up YouTube videos explaining the same concepts because different explanations helped. None of that showed up in the system.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours going over my handwritten notes or practicing concepts on paper, especially for things that required hands-on understanding. I'd also study with a friend from the peer support group in person, working through problems together. The LMS would just show I hadn't logged in, but I was actually trying to learn.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or PDF once, then work with it offline for days. My internet at home isn't always reliable and I have limited data, so downloading and studying offline was more practical. The system probably thought I watched something for a few minutes when really I studied it for hours offline.
Q12: Around midterms everything fell apart for me. My work increased my hours, my anxiety spiked, and I felt like I was drowning in all six courses. I started missing classes and avoiding the LMS because even looking at it stressed me out. The workload felt impossible and I just shut down for a while.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, the last thing I wanted to do was log into the LMS and face all the things I was behind on. Confusion about course material made me avoid it too because I didn't want to feel stupid. It became this cycle where avoiding it made me more behind, which made me more anxious.
Q14: So many times. I'd log in, click through some pages, maybe start a video, but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd be worrying about bills or just too tired to actually absorb anything. I'd technically be 'engaging' with materials but nothing was sinking in. It was just going through motions.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, especially the big ones that felt overwhelming. My anxiety made it hard to start things because I was afraid of doing them wrong. I knew they were important but the fear of failure paralyzed me. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow and then tomorrow would come with the same avoidance.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me engage. I'd ignore the LMS for days until something was due soon, then frantically try to catch up. It wasn't healthy but it was the only way I functioned. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have done anything at all.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it could go either way. Positive feedback made me feel like maybe I could do this. Negative or critical feedback sometimes made me spiral and avoid the course even more because I felt like I was already failing. The feedback came too late sometimes to actually help me improve on the next thing.
Q18: Having six courses meant I couldn't give proper attention to any of them. The structure of this particular course assumed I had more time than I actually did. Weekly modules piling up made me feel constantly behind. A more flexible structure might have helped me manage better.
Q19: The peer support aspect actually made engagement easier because it felt less isolating. But the amount of content and the pace made it harder. Having to watch long videos without any interactive elements was difficult for someone like me who learns by doing. More hands-on activities would have helped.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who logs in, clicks around trying to figure things out, maybe starts assignments but doesn't finish them. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes you're just lost and clicking through things trying to understand what you're supposed to do. Or you're too anxious to actually complete and submit work.
Q21: That makes sense to me because so much learning happens outside the system. Someone could be studying from downloaded materials, meeting with study groups, using textbooks, and doing really well without the LMS showing it. The LMS only captures one slice of how people learn.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you actually understood anything or if you were just going through motions. They miss offline studying, emotional state, life circumstances, and whether the engagement was meaningful. They're just surface-level data.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to it somehow. It's not just showing up or clicking through things. For me, real engagement would be when I'm curious and want to understand, not just trying to survive until the deadline. It's hard to feel that way when you're overwhelmed.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I was actually trying despite everything working against me. It wouldn't show my anxiety attacks before assignments or how much I studied offline. It wouldn't show that peer support genuinely helped my mood even when my grades didn't reflect it. It just sees numbers, not the person struggling behind them.
